item.title: Fijian among three escaped prisoners sought in Tonga
item.description: Tonga Police are appealing for public assistance to locate three escaped prisoners, including one man identified as the holder of a Fijian passport. In a public alert, police named the escapee Adam Zabid Hussein, who is identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.
item.content:encoded:
Tonga Police are appealing for public assistance to locate three escaped prisoners, including one man identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.

In a public alert, police named the escapee Adam Zabid Hussein, who is identified as the holder of a Fijian passport.

Authorities have urged members of the public not to approach the escapees if they are seen, warning that they should instead immediately contact emergency number 922 or the nearest police station with any information about their whereabouts.

Tonga Police also reminded the public that it is a criminal offence to knowingly assist, harbour, conceal or provide any form of aid to escaped prisoners.

The force thanked the public for its continued cooperation, vigilance and support as efforts continue to locate the three men.

Police said anyone with information that may assist the investigation is urged to contact Tonga Police immediately.

No further details have been released on how or when the prisoners escaped, and investigations are continuing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Rain to spread across Fiji from later today
item.description: Rain is expected to spread across Fiji from the west later today, with forecasters warning of isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms across most of the country by Wednesday. The National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi states a trough with associated cloud and rain, currently lying west of Fiji, is gradually moving eastwards and is expected to affect the group later today.
item.content:encoded:
Rain is expected to spread across Fiji from the west later today, with forecasters warning of isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms across most of the country by Wednesday.

The National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi states a trough with associated cloud and rain, currently lying west of Fiji, is gradually moving eastwards and is expected to affect the group later today.

Before the rain arrives, cloudy periods and some showers are forecast for Serua-Namosi, Navua-Suva-Nausori, Tailevu-Naitasiri-Ra, southern Bua, Cakaudrove, Udu Point, Taveuni and nearby islands, the Lau and Lomaiviti groups, Kadavu and nearby islands, while the rest of the country is expected to remain mainly fine.

The weather office says conditions will be cool overnight, with moderate easterly winds, fresh at times, and moderate seas.

The outlook for Wednesday is for occasional rain with isolated heavy falls and thunderstorms over most parts of Fiji as the trough moves across the country.

Authorities are advising the public to monitor the latest weather updates, particularly those in flood-prone areas, as heavy rain could lead to localised flooding.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Electoral reform proposal ‘mathematically unworkable’, report warns
item.description: A technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s proposed electoral system has concluded that the model is “not a workable proportional electoral system”, warning it contains fundamental mathematical flaws that could undermine election results. In the 13-page assessment prepared by Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal, the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency Sys
item.content:encoded:
A technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s proposed electoral system has concluded that the model is “not a workable proportional electoral system”, warning it contains fundamental mathematical flaws that could undermine election results.

In the 13-page assessment prepared by Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal, the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency System (POLCS) is described as “mathematically incomplete, internally contradictory and incapable of reliably delivering its central promise of national proportionality.”

The report argues the proposal’s central formula—calculating list seats by subtracting constituency seats from a party’s overall entitlement—fails whenever a party wins more constituency seats than its share of the national vote justifies.

Using a hypothetical example, the assessment states that if a party won all 50 constituency seats with just 51 per cent of the national vote, it would be entitled to only about 36 seats under proportional representation.

The formula would therefore require the party to receive minus 14 MPs, something the report says is mathematically impossible.

According to the assessment, only three outcomes would then be possible: remove elected constituency MPs, abandon proportional representation, or significantly increase the size of Parliament through “overhang” and levelling seats.

It says none of those solutions is explained in the proposal.

The report also criticises the proposed ratio of 50 constituency seats and only 21 compensatory list seats, saying it gives far too much weight to first-past-the-post elections before proportionality is considered.

It argues that New Zealand’s MMP system uses a much safer electorate-to-list ratio, whereas Fiji’s proposed 70:30 split creates a much greater risk of disproportional election outcomes.

The assessment concludes that unless a detailed overhang mechanism exists but has not been published, the proposal cannot guarantee proportional representation despite claims that it would.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PCC warns against militarising Pacific’s ‘Ocean of Peace’ vision
item.description: The General Secretary of the Pacific Conference of Churches (PCC), Reverend James Bhagwan, has urged Pacific leaders to ensure the region’s “Ocean of Peace” vision is not overtaken by military and security interests, warning that peace must remain rooted in justice, dialogue and the wellbeing of Pacific peoples. In a seven-page pastoral open letter titled “Let Peace Govern Security”, Rev. Bhagwan
item.content:encoded:
The General Secretary of the Pacific Conference of Churches (PCC), Reverend James Bhagwan, has urged Pacific leaders to ensure the region’s “Ocean of Peace” vision is not overtaken by military and security interests, warning that peace must remain rooted in justice, dialogue and the wellbeing of Pacific peoples.

In a seven-page pastoral open letter titled “Let Peace Govern Security”, Rev. Bhagwan reflects on the biblical passage of Micah 4:1-4 and calls on Pacific governments, churches and international partners to preserve the original intent of the Ocean of Peace initiative.

Writing from Majuro in the Marshall Islands, Rev. Bhagwan says Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka’s Ocean of Peace vision was initially welcomed because it offered the Pacific a way to promote peace through relationships, dignity, dialogue and care for creation rather than geopolitical competition.

However, he expresses concern that the concept is increasingly being linked to defence and security arrangements.

Referring to the recently signed Veitacini Treaty between Fiji and Australia, Rev. Bhagwan notes that while the agreement affirms dialogue and respect for sovereignty, it also includes provisions on deterrence, collective defence and responses to armed attacks.

He points to growing public debate over whether the Ocean of Peace vision has been “hijacked” and cites concerns raised by regional civil society leaders who fear the concept is being co-opted by external military interests.

Rev. Bhagwan argues that the Pacific’s understanding of peace must remain broader than national security.

He says Pacific churches and communities have long defined peace as the wellbeing of people, land and ocean, grounded in reconciliation, justice, neighbourliness and respect for Indigenous knowledge.

The letter also recalls the legacy of nuclear testing in the Marshall Islands, ongoing climate change, violence against women, deep-sea mining and economic inequality as some of the region’s greatest security challenges.

Rev. Bhagwan calls for the proposed Ocean of Peace Centre to focus on conflict prevention, mediation, trauma healing, social cohesion and civilian-led peacebuilding rather than military objectives.

He also urges leaders to ensure women, young people, persons with disabilities, Indigenous communities, churches and civil society share decision-making authority from the outset.

Concluding the letter, Rev. Bhagwan says security should always be guided by peace rather than the reverse.

“May peace give security its direction. May justice shape every agreement,” he writes, calling on Pacific leaders to choose dialogue over confrontation and ensure the Ocean of Peace remains under the shared guardianship of Pacific peoples.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Medical supplies to boost Fiji’s healthcare capacity
item.description: A donation of 1,283 medical items, including 170 hospital beds, has arrived in Fiji following a 14-month international collaboration aimed at strengthening the country’s public healthcare system. Reach 4 Your Future Foundation Inc. announced that the first phase of the project has been completed, with approximately 26 tonnes of hospital equipment transported from the former Footscray Hospital in M
item.content:encoded:
A donation of 1,283 medical items, including 170 hospital beds, has arrived in Fiji following a 14-month international collaboration aimed at strengthening the country’s public healthcare system.

Reach 4 Your Future Foundation Inc. announced that the first phase of the project has been completed, with approximately 26 tonnes of hospital equipment transported from the former Footscray Hospital in Melbourne to Fiji in eight 40-foot shipping containers.

The Foundation said the project was the result of 14 months of negotiations between Medical Pantry Founder Dr Martin Nguyen and Reach 4 Your Future Foundation Founder and President Ganesh Sen, who worked with the Western Health Decommissioning Team to recover serviceable hospital assets from the decommissioned Australian facility.

The donation includes hospital beds, patient observation equipment, emergency and medication carts, surgical and clinical care aids, mobility equipment, waiting-room seating and hospital furniture.

According to the Foundation, the 170 hospital beds alone are expected to address approximately one-third of Fiji’s identified hospital bed capacity gap, providing a significant boost to the country’s public health services.

The project received FJ$137,000 in logistics funding from the New Zealand Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade (MFAT), which covered international ocean freight and associated transport costs.

Swire Shipping Agencies Fiji and Core Logistics Fiji coordinated the transport of the equipment from Melbourne to Fiji.

In Fiji, Reach 4 Your Future Foundation representative Lanoma Singh worked closely with the Ministry of Health and Medical Services to ensure the donated equipment aligned with national healthcare priorities.

Following the shipment’s arrival, the Ministry of Health and Medical Services took responsibility for customs clearance before beginning the inspection, allocation and distribution of the equipment across Fiji’s public hospital network.

The Foundation said the initiative brought together Western Health, Medical Pantry, Reach 4 Your Future Foundation, New Zealand MFAT, Swire Shipping, Core Logistics Fiji and the Ministry of Health and Medical Services in a partnership that prevented valuable medical assets from being discarded while extending their use to benefit patients and healthcare workers in Fiji.

The Foundation said the next phase of the project will focus on inspecting, allocating and distributing the equipment to hospitals throughout the country.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Dialogue Fiji says current voting system more proportional
item.description: Dialogue Fiji says Fiji’s current electoral system delivers a more accurate reflection of voters’ choices than the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency System being considered during constitutional reform. In its technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s recommendations, Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal argues the existing Open List Proportional Represen
item.content:encoded:
Dialogue Fiji says Fiji’s current electoral system delivers a more accurate reflection of voters’ choices than the proposed Proportional Open List Constituency System being considered during constitutional reform.

In its technical assessment of the Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission’s recommendations, Dialogue Fiji Executive Director Nilesh Lal argues the existing Open List Proportional Representation (OLPR) system produced an “exceptionally proportional” outcome in the 2022 General Election.

The report shows FijiFirst secured 46.05 per cent of qualifying votes and 47.27 per cent of parliamentary seats, while the People’s Alliance received 38.77 per cent of votes and 38.18 per cent of seats.

The National Federation Party obtained 9.62 per cent of votes and 9.09 per cent of seats, while SODELPA received 5.56 per cent of votes and 5.45 per cent of seats.

According to the assessment, the largest difference between vote share and seat share was only 1.22 percentage points, demonstrating that the current system translated votes into parliamentary representation with a high degree of accuracy.

By comparison, the report argues the proposed POLCS cannot guarantee proportional representation because around 70 per cent of parliamentary seats would already be allocated through constituency contests before national proportionality is calculated.

It also warns that the proposed model could increase wasted votes, make constituency boundaries politically significant again, favour geographically concentrated parties over dispersed support, and potentially require Parliament to expand if overhang seats are created.

The report concludes that while the proposal promises local MPs, greater women’s representation and simpler voting, many of those objectives conflict with one another.

Instead, it argues the existing OLPR system has one key strength—every vote cast anywhere in Fiji carries equal value in determining the composition of Parliament—and says that principle should not be abandoned without a technically sound alternative.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Chocolates, suki, meth and phones among prison contraband seized
item.description: A package containing marijuana, methamphetamine, suki, mobile phones, chocolates and snacks was intercepted after being thrown into the Suva Corrections Centre in Walu Bay this morning. The Fiji Corrections Service (FCS) said alert officers on duty intercepted the illegal package at about 11am, when inmates were moving from their dormitories to the kitchen mess hall for lunch.
item.content:encoded:
A package containing marijuana, methamphetamine, suki, mobile phones, chocolates and snacks was intercepted after being thrown into the Suva Corrections Centre in Walu Bay this morning.

The Fiji Corrections Service (FCS) said alert officers on duty intercepted the illegal package at about 11am, when inmates were moving from their dormitories to the kitchen mess hall for lunch.

According to FCS, the suspects deliberately targeted the prison’s lunch routine in an apparent attempt to smuggle contraband into the facility.

Among the items seized were 17 rolls of marijuana, two sachets of methamphetamine crystals and 45 rolls of suki.

Officers also recovered one touch-screen mobile phone, one button mobile phone, one thumb-sized phone and a charging cable.

The package further contained two packets of chocolate, two packets of chewing gum, two packets of Sunrise Bongo bean snacks and a gas lighter.

The Fiji Corrections Service said its surveillance systems and CCTV cameras captured images of the suspects and recorded their vehicle registration details.

Acting Commissioner of Corrections Auta Moceisuva condemned the incident, saying it undermined efforts to rehabilitate inmates.

“This is a prime example of the lack of support from members of the public who continue to fuel this illegal activity. We have identified the culprits through our security systems and CCTV, recorded their car registration numbers, and are now working with the Police to bring them to justice,” he said.

The Fiji Corrections Service is working with the Fiji Police Force to locate and arrest those involved.

FCS reiterated its zero-tolerance policy on prison contraband and said it will continue strengthening surveillance and security measures to prevent illegal items from entering correctional facilities.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Mortuary holds Mister X after DNA tests fail
item.description: THE Lautoka Hospital mortuary is holding an unidentified middle aged iTaukei man nicknamed Mister X whose body has remained unclaimed for four months. Due to his remains being in an advanced stage of decomposition when authorities recovered him, forensic identification has been close to impossible.
item.content:encoded:
THE Lautoka Hospital mortuary is holding an unidentified middle aged iTaukei man nicknamed Mister X whose body has remained unclaimed for four months.

Due to his remains being in an advanced stage of decomposition when authorities recovered him, forensic identification has been close to impossible.

Tagimoucia Funeral Services director Shalvin Rohit Krishna said DNA tests on the deceased have failed to provide a positive identification.

“Currently the DNA tests have been done on him but there have been no results,” said Mr Krishna.

“For these cases, the first thing done is DNA tests are taken and matched with missing persons reports.

“So far for Mr X, there has been no positive results.”

Mr Krishna said other bodies that have remained unclaimed at the morgue included foetuses.

“We also have two foetuses, and two baby heads that were found in Field 4 in Naikabula.

“For these types of cases they are handled by police, and they are kept at the mortuary under their supervision.”

Mr Krishna said they have been working closely with authorities to carry out burials of unclaimed bodies.

His staff with a police escort cremated a 21-year-old man who had been at the morgue since October, 2024.

“It is quite difficult to do these sorts of things ourselves because there is a lot of legal processes to follow.

“If it was up to us, I would bury these unclaimed bodies if they have been here for too long.”

Minister for Social Protection Sashi Kiran said they were saddened by the reports of the unclaimed bodies.

“We are very sad to know that there are foetuses which means they have been babies dumped after delivery, but we do need to understand a little bit more about that such as finding out what happened to the mothers,” she said.

“I want to say that we are very sad to note that a country that calls itself a very communal and spiritual country is not able to take charge in this matter.

“Every person has a family, a village or a community and I plead with the community and the country that if somebody passes on and even though they have their differences it is important that they at least get a peaceful rest and it has to be done by families and communities.

“And if they are struggling to do it, they can reach out to us for help, but it is sad to hear that there are many unclaimed lives that have contributed in their own way to the nation.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
